nodes.title: Loads on the System
nodes.note:
CIRA wants an Application Ecology Layer 🌿

Reducer/Core + CIRA gives you an excellent pattern for one coherent editable world:

state → action → reducer → render
plus undo/redo, inspection, persistence, and canvas manipulation.

But now you’re trying to build systems where there are:

* multiple windows
* summonable tools
* hypertextual navigation
* component reuse
* LLM-generated submodules
* graphical tutorials
* runtime testing
* configuration/storage/app lifecycle concerns
* maybe one global state, maybe many local states

That’s not a failure of CIRA. That’s a sign that CIRA is a lower layer, and you now need a layer above it.

nodes.title: Does tkinter support declaring that a given tkinter.Toplevel is modal?
TkAppFrame / Application Ecology Layer
nodes.note:
It owns things like:

* window registry
* summonable windows
* persistent app storage
* configuration
* runtime services
* testing gateway
* LLM-friendly module contracts
* tutorial overlays
* window lifecycle
* inter-window messaging
* tick/update loop


Application services:
* config
* storage
* logging
* command dispatch
* runtime tick
* window registry
* event bus
* test gateway
* tutorial overlay registry

Window services
* summon window
* close window
* focus window
* find existing instance
* register widgets
* register tutorial targets
* register test handles

CIRA services?
* create reducer core
* attach undo/redo
* route Ctrl+Z / Ctrl+Y
* serialize document state
* dispatch action
* observe state changes

nodes.title: Window Summoning Policy
nodes.note:
Every window definition should declare its summon policy.

Something like:

WINDOW_DEF = {
    "WINDOW_TYPE": "asset_browser",
    "TITLE": "Asset Browser",
    "SUMMON_POLICY": "singleton",
}

or:

WINDOW_DEF = {
    "WINDOW_TYPE": "object_inspector",
    "TITLE": "Inspector",
    "SUMMON_POLICY": "per-key",
    "KEY_KIND": "object_guid",
}

Then the app frame handles it.

Possible summon policies:

Policy	Meaning
"singleton"	Only one instance exists. Summon brings it forward.
"per-key"	One instance per key, such as object GUID, document path, asset ID.
"free"	Every summon creates a new window.
"embedded"	Not a Toplevel; can be mounted inside another frame.
"modal"	Temporary blocking interaction window.


This gives you the exact thing you wanted: one imported window definition can behave correctly in different apps, because it publishes how it expects to be summoned.

---

This part matters because it determines lifecycle and storage behavior.

Examples:

Window	Policy
Main window	singleton
Asset browser	singleton
Object inspector	per-key
Search results	free or per-query
Preferences	singleton or modal
Rename dialog	modal
Embedded toolbar	embedded
Embedded property pane	embedded

nodes.title: Tutorial Overlays
nodes.note:
"But you can still get 80% of the effect inside a normal Tk app."

For each Toplevel, create a transparent-ish overlay Toplevel or Canvas layer that sits above the window.

You can use:

winfo_rootx()
winfo_rooty()
winfo_width()
winfo_height()

to locate widgets on screen, then draw highlight boxes/arrows relative to them.

This supports things like:

Dim this window.
Highlight the Tags field.
Draw arrow to the Hook button.
Show explanatory bubble.
Wait for user click.

nodes.title: First Build Goals
nodes.note:
What you are targeting today

Your present target is the minimum viable TkVillage kernel.

Not CIRA itself.
Not tutorial overlays.
Not a visual builder.
Not a full plugin system.
Not a game engine.

You are building the host organism.

TkVillage Kernel

It should include:

Subsystem	Purpose
App object / global app dictionary	shared runtime context
project directory	.foo/ style local app data root
configuration system	named, typed config parameters
CLI interface	inspect/set config, run tests, launch app
window registry	register window modules
summon system	open/focus/create windows by name/key
window policies	singleton, per-key, free, embedded, modal
lifecycle hooks	create, show, close, destroy
tick loop	periodic runtime heartbeat
message bus	inter-window/module communication
testing gateway	run app in test mode inside tkinter mainloop
LLM module contract	simple documented pattern for window modules

That’s enough. That’s already a coherent first architecture.

nodes.title: Envisioned Initial Code
nodes.note:
import tkvillage as village
import windows.main_window as main_window
import windows.asset_browser as asset_browser
import windows.object_inspector as object_inspector

g = {
    "quit": False,
}

def main():
    app = village.make_app(
        app_name="data_garden",
        project_dir=".data_garden",
        g=g,
    )

    village.define_config(app, "autosave_seconds", int, 10)
    village.define_config(app, "theme", str, "default")

    village.register_window(app, main_window.WINDOW_DEF)
    village.register_window(app, asset_browser.WINDOW_DEF)
    village.register_window(app, object_inspector.WINDOW_DEF)

    village.run_cli_or_app(app)


---

my feedback:
* The declarative dimension of this -- that's correct.
* But it's not object oriented.  At all.
* And you don't call make_app, because, dufus,
  if you're using village, there's an APP.
  You just use the global space, because all tkvillage
  programs are apps using the tkvillage.
* The tkvillage system should
  have it's own g["quit"] just by default, defaulting to False.
  You don't have to specify that.
  Why make the user do something that they could get wrong, for no reason?

I would expect more like:


---
import tkvillage as village
import windows.main_window as main_window
import windows.asset_browser as asset_browser
import windows.object_inspector as object_inspector

def setup_app():
    village.define_app({"name": "data_garden",
                        "title": "Data Garden",
                        "dir": ".data_garden"})
    village.define_config({"name": "autosave_seconds",
                           "type": "int",
                           "title": "Autosave (in seconds)",
                           "default": "10"})
    village.register_module(mainwindow)
    village.register_module(asset_browser)
    village.register_module(object_inspector)
    
    village.run()

---

register_module would then locate the WINDOW_DEF within it.

village.run() will read the command line arguments, and determine
whether to:

(a) present help information (and if so, what help information)
(b) process the command and print response (for example, "datagarden set autosave_seconds 10" will just respond with an "Affirmative," and confirmation that autosave_seconds has been set to 10)
(c) start the GUI and run the program
(d) start the GUI and run test routines

nodes.title: Shape of a Window Module
nodes.note:
OK, so Wing-Cat Proposed:

---
WINDOW_DEF = {
    "NAME": "object_inspector",
    "TITLE": "Object Inspector",
    "POLICY": "per-key",
    "KEY_NAME": "object_guid",
    "REQUIRES": ["storage", "messages"],
    "PUBLISHES": ["object-updated"],
    "SUBSCRIBES": ["selection-changed"],
}

def create(app, parent, key=None):
    ...
    return {
        "root": toplevel_or_frame,
        "close": close,
        "tick": tick,
        "focus": focus,
    }
---

My thoughts:

* REQUIRES/PUBLISHES/SUBSCRIBES -- this is all going to depend on the communication system, which I am uncommitted to at this time.

Dictated:
---
Remember that we're not using object-oriented programming. Everything is declarative. Uh, when a event is hit on a window, like say a menu item is chosen, the mouse moves through it, um, wherever an event occurs, it's going to pass through the village's event handler, the TK village events handler. And the TK village's event handler is going to identify which window the event appeared on, and it's going to activate the window's data structures. What I mean by activating it is just that there will be some global pointers that say, this is the window that is the window of concern here. It should be possible to get to all the data for all the windows that aren't active, uh, but when the event handler within the user's module code for that window is hit, uh, the TK village system will have already activated that window. It's not going to pass it. Uh, so when the handler responds, it should always assume, uh, when the end handler responds, it should always assume that TK village has already put the poke into, you know, its registers or whatever, the current address for the current window. This matters a lot because you're gonna have window code that is, has the policy that it's per key, right? And so when that event handler is running, the village system will have already poked in the right object GUID and the right pointers to the window data structures. So, you know, but it's not object-oriented. That's really critical to understand. It's contextually based. Uh, and remember, um, these methods, these functions, they take at most two parameters and optionally some flags. Uh, so typically they'll just receive, there'll be nullary, which means we're just relying on the context to tell us everything about the situation, or they'll have like one parameter. And remember, my conviction is that a parameter is something that actually meaningfully changes. I don't use parameters to convey configuration information. That's what the context is for. So that applies to this system as well.  Maybe even: especially.
---

nodes.title: Process Notes
nodes.note:
Define the Following:

* App -- title, name, description, project folder, ...
* Project Folder structure
* Configuration Data -- configuration options, titles, identifiers, types, defaults, storage location, ...
* Window Declaration & Invocation -- how you declare a window, how you create a window
* Inter-Module Communications System -- what model defines communication, how messages are delivered, ...
* Run-time Core Loop

village.project_path(app, "config.json")
village.storage_path(app, "objects", "abc123.json")
village.ensure_project_dirs(app)
village.read_json(app, "storage/foo.json")
village.write_json(app, "storage/foo.json", data)
---

nodes.title: Envisioned Configuration Declaration
nodes.note:
Wing-Cat's Vision:

---
define_config(
    app,
    name="autosave_seconds",
    type=int,
    default=10,
    description="Seconds between autosave ticks.",
)
---

Wing-Cat says to choose a naming convention.
I agree with Wing-Cat's proposal:
  skewer-case, all lowercase

  "foo-bar-baz"

nodes.title: Originating Discussion: tkinter Conventions
nodes.note:
This conversation began from a different place, actually --
a discussion of common tkinter conventions.

And it evolved into: "Well, we should make a framework out of this."

Interestingly enough, what came out looked almost EXACTLY like the First Build Goals..!
